evidence: Contract identity primitives are re-declared in multiple modules: `ProtocolActionContractRoot` and upload/protocol action constants appear in both `goseg/protocol/contracts/protocolfamily/specs.go` and `goseg/uploadsvc/contractspec/specs.go` instead of flowing from one canonical boundary.

Cross-package alignment is enforced by tests (`goseg/protocol/contracts/conformance/fixtures_test.go`, `goseg/uploadsvc/contractspec/contractspec_test.go`) that compare counts and mappings across registries, indicating architectural drift risk already exists and is being caught after-the-fact.

Family spec types are fragmented (`ActionSpec`, `UploadActionSpec`, `ContractSpec`) with different fields and semantics across `protocolfamily`, `uploadsvc/contractspec`, and `startramfamily`, which weakens shared contract boundaries and makes consistency dependent on manual synchronization.

Runtime dependency modeling is heavily callback-bag based across startup/importer/orchestration: `startBackgroundServicesRuntime` + `startupInitRuntime` in `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go`, `importerRuntime` and projected runtime variants in `goseg/importer/importer.go`, and many `Runtime*Ops` structs in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go`.

`goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go` defines a large `startBackgroundServiceSpecs` table where each spec mostly returns one field from the same runtime bag via near-identical closures, which adds indirection without meaningful policy separation.

`goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go` embeds multiple broad orchestration bundles (`RuntimeContainerOps`, `RuntimeUrbitOps`, `RuntimeSnapshotOps`) into one workflow runtime, creating wide dependency surfaces for callers.

Global default runtimes are used as hidden shared dependencies (`defaultBroadcastStateRuntime` in `goseg/broadcast/state.go`, `defaultDockerSubsystemRuntime` in `goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go`, `defaultEventRuntime` in `goseg/docker/events/events.go`), which weakens abstraction honesty and makes boundary ownership less explicit.

All four package docs use near-identical rhetorical framing: "It intentionally does not own ... so ... remains ..." in goseg/shipworkflow/doc.go, goseg/startram/doc.go, goseg/system/doc.go, and goseg/uploadsvc/doc.go.
The comments are predominantly boundary disclaimers rather than concrete operational guidance, despite these being short package-level docs with no migration state markers.
Phrasing is formulaic across unrelated domains (ship workflow, StarTram, system, upload service), which matches LLM-style template reuse and increases maintenance burden when boundaries evolve.

holistic_context.structure.directory_profiles shows very large flat directories for this batch scope: goseg/shipworkflow/ has 53 files, goseg/config/ has 41 files, and goseg/docker/orchestration/ has 34 files (all above the >10 mixed-concern split threshold).

goseg/shipworkflow/lifecycle.go imports only groundseg/shipworkflow/lifecyclewait and re-exports wrappers/aliases, indicating near-total import-affinity to a sibling concern while living in the parent package.

goseg/config/bootstrap.go contains startup/bootstrap path/debug initialization and singleton lifecycle concerns, while goseg/config/config.go is runtime snapshot/state facade code; both sit in one broad package without boundary-focused subfolders.

goseg/docker/orchestration/container_bridge.go and container_config.go are container-runtime composition/adaptation code but are placed at orchestration root alongside unrelated orchestration concerns, reducing navigability.
`family_specs_test.go` builds synthetic `contractFamilyEntrySpec` values inline instead of validating real outputs from `protocolfamily.ActionSpecs()` and `startramfamily.ContractSpecs()`.
`fixtures_test.go` validates fixture/binding consistency but does not assert that family spec declarations remain synchronized with canonical bindings and governance metadata.
`uploadsvc/actions_test.go` validates parser behavior only; it does not test cross-module compatibility from parsed action -> contract binding -> family spec governance.

Configuration shape is modeled in parallel across `structs.SysConfig` section structs (`goseg/structs/configs.go`) and another full patch schema with mirrored fields (`ConnectivityPatch`, `RuntimePatch`, `StartramPatch`, `PenpaiPatch`, `AuthSessionPatch`) in `goseg/config/config_update_schema.go`.

`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go` centralizes many unrelated seams (container lifecycle, startup bootstrap/load, startram, urbit, snapshots, file/image/volume/timer/wireguard/netdata/minio), creating one broad composition surface rather than cohesive runtime modules.

`goseg/broadcast/state.go` combines mutable state storage, scheduling, collector wiring, cloning/serialization helpers, and delivery coordination in one runtime object; `goseg/session/logstream_session_store.go` similarly combines system sessions, docker sessions, and an event bus in one store type.

Large test files rely on bespoke local harness layers and repeated runtime/setup scaffolding (`goseg/importer/importer_test.go`, `goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint_test.go`, `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator_test.go`) instead of shared scenario builders.

shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go mixes API styles: some entrypoints accept explicit overrides (DispatchUploadImportWithCoordinator, RunTransitionedOperationWithRuntime) while adjacent public functions always allocate defaults internally (PublishTransitionWithPolicy, WaitComplete).
broadcast/loop.go exposes StartBroadcastLoopWithRuntime but StopBroadcastLoop has only the global-default path, so lifecycle control is asymmetric for non-default runtimes.
docker/events/events.go similarly keeps both NewEventRuntime(overrides...) and DefaultEventRuntime(), reinforcing mixed global-vs-injected API expectations.
structs/configs.go uses mixed acronym/casing conventions in one model surface: ApiVersion, WgOn/WgRegistered, EndpointURL, LastKnownMDNS, and CfgDir.
structs/configs.go mixes JSON naming styles in the same persistence file (camelCase fields, snake_case urbit fields, and an uppercase outlier json:"CFG_DIR").
defaults/defaults.go and config/config.go consume these types directly, so naming/style drift propagates across config and runtime call sites.
uploadsvc/service_test.go asserts exact error string content for command validation and propagation, indicating stringly error contracts rather than stable typed/sentinel errors.
protocol/actions/actions_test.go validates typed errors via errors.As (UnsupportedActionError), showing a different and stronger contract style in adjacent action APIs.
system/wifi_test.go uses errors.Is with sentinel ErrWiFiInterfaceNotFound, introducing a third contract style across related service boundaries.
broadcast/state.go creates a process-wide mutable singleton (defaultBroadcastStateRuntime) returned by DefaultBroadcastStateRuntime().
broadcast/loop.go relies on process-global defaultBroadcastLoopController and StopBroadcastLoop() only controls that global controller.

docker/events/events.go (sync.OnceValue defaultEventRuntime) and protocol/actions/actions.go (sync.Once + package-level caches) keep initialization and mutable runtime state in package globals tied to first-use order.
docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go uses inconsistent seam naming within the same runtime surfaces (Initialize vs UpdateConfigTypedFn vs LoadMCFn/LoadMinIOsFn).
startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go uses callback fields with explicit Fn suffixes, but neighboring runtime APIs in orchestration omit or mix this convention.
structs/configs.go abbreviations vary by symbol (ApiVersion, Wg*, CfgDir, MDNS), which weakens intent clarity and searchability.

findings.summary: Protocol contract IDs/namespaces are duplicated across modules instead of owned by one boundary.
evidence: `protocolfamily/specs.go` defines `ProtocolActionContractRoot`, namespace/action constants, and concatenated IDs locally.
`uploadsvc/contractspec/specs.go` independently defines the same root/namespace/action-ID composition pattern for upload contracts.
`contractspec_test.go` and `fixtures_test.go` perform cross-registry alignment checks, showing modules are synchronized by tests rather than by a single source-of-truth API.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single canonical contract identity registry/API in `protocol/contracts` (typed IDs + binding metadata), then make `protocolfamily` and `uploadsvc/contractspec` consume it directly. Remove duplicated root/namespace/action literals from leaf modules and keep only module-specific descriptors where necessary.

findings.summary: Contract-family packages expose incompatible spec schemas, creating boundary drift between sibling modules.
evidence: `protocolfamily.ActionSpec` and `uploadsvc/contractspec.UploadActionSpec` use action-binding fields (`Namespace`, `Action`, `ContractID`) while `startramfamily.ContractSpec` uses a different shape (`ID`, `Name`, `Description`, `Message`, `Owner`) with no shared contract model.

`family_specs_test.go` validates governance and family-entry behavior via `contractFamilyEntrySpec`, but those governance fields are not represented in the sibling family spec packages, so cross-module consistency is not enforced at the model boundary.
Because sibling packages define different schema contracts, any new metadata requirement must be propagated manually across modules, increasing coupling and drift risk.
findings.suggestion: Define a shared family-spec contract in `protocol/contracts` (core fields + optional extension points), and migrate `protocolfamily`, `startramfamily`, and `uploadsvc/contractspec` to implement/compose that shared model. Add conversion helpers in one place rather than package-specific ad hoc schemas.

findings.summary: Runtime callback bags have expanded into low-cohesion dependency surfaces.
evidence: `runtime_ops.go` defines many broad bags (`RuntimeContainerOps`, `RuntimeSnapshotOps`, `RuntimeUrbitOps`, etc.) and then re-bundles them via seam builders.
`workflowRuntime` in `operations_runtime.go` embeds three large orchestration bags, so operations inherit more callbacks than most individual flows need.
`importer.go` models storage/upload/provision runtimes plus multiple projection types and requirement-specific resolvers, indicating abstraction spread rather than tight boundaries.
`startup_orchestrator.go` carries several nested runtime structs with many callback fields across init/background phases.
findings.suggestion: Replace broad callback bags with capability-scoped interfaces per use-case (for example: transition emitter, container lifecycle, ship config mutation, import publish). Build per-flow dependencies explicitly instead of embedding large ops structs; keep each flow's constructor accepting only the capabilities it consumes.

findings.summary: Startup service spec abstraction mostly forwards fields and duplicates mapping logic.
evidence: `startBackgroundServiceSpecs` in `startup_orchestrator.go` contains many entries whose `startFn` closure only returns one runtime field.
`startBackgroundServiceCallbackSetters` in `startup_orchestrator_test.go` mirrors this mapping with another large name-to-setter table, showing the same indirection cost replicated in tests.
findings.suggestion: Collapse the two-step mapping into a direct `map[startupBackgroundServiceName]func(context.Context) error` built from the resolved runtime once. Keep `failFast/requiresWg` metadata separate, and let tests override that map directly instead of maintaining a parallel setter registry.

findings.summary: Importer runtime projections add wrapper layers with little behavioral leverage.
evidence: `importer.go` has multiple resolver/projection functions (`resolveImporterRuntimeForWorkflow`, `resolveImporterRuntimeForUpload`, `resolveImporterRuntimeForPublish`, `resolveImporterRuntimeForInit`) plus `buildImporter*Runtime` helpers that mostly slice/forward fields.
Workflows then repeatedly resolve runtime variants before simple operations, creating extra call depth around otherwise direct behavior.
findings.suggestion: Converge on one validated runtime per execution path and pass narrowed interfaces directly to helpers. Remove projection builders that only copy fields; where validation differs, use one validator with explicit capability flags and keep the runtime shape stable.

findings.summary: Global default runtime singletons weaken explicit dependency boundaries.
evidence: `state.go` exposes process-wide `defaultBroadcastStateRuntime` and multiple APIs route through it when explicit runtime is omitted.
`subsystem_events.go` uses `defaultDockerSubsystemRuntime` as implicit shared state for health/event loops.
`events.go` uses `defaultEventRuntime` via `sync.OnceValue`, allowing silent fallback to a global broker.
findings.suggestion: Keep global defaults only at composition root and require explicit runtime/event bus injection for package-level operations. Introduce constructors that return service instances and deprecate package functions that implicitly capture singleton state.

findings.summary: Package docs use repeated AI-style boundary disclaimer templates across modules
evidence: Each file repeats the same template structure: capability list, then "intentionally does not own ..." boundary caveat.

The repeated wording appears copy-patterned rather than domain-specific explanation, e.g., "so this package can stay focused...", "so ... logic remains integration-oriented", "to keep this package as the boundary-facing facade."
The docs do not include concrete migration markers (what moved, where, or when) even though they frame ownership boundaries as if documenting migration outcomes.
findings.suggestion: Rewrite package docs to be domain-specific and concise: keep one sentence on responsibility, one sentence on explicit dependency boundary, and include concrete references to actual subpackages/APIs (or migration notes with actionable identifiers) instead of reusable disclaimer prose.

findings.summary: Doc comments are verbose relative to content and mostly restate packaging intent
evidence: The assigned files are minimal package declarations where comment text dominates file content.
Most clauses describe what the package "does not own" instead of giving precise behavior contracts or invariants.
Across files, wording varies superficially but communicates the same generic ownership trope, reducing signal-to-noise.
findings.suggestion: Trim each doc.go to high-signal content only: specific responsibilities, key entrypoints, and one concrete non-obvious boundary rule; remove generic negative-ownership phrasing unless it is tied to a named contract or migration artifact.

findings.summary: The config package is an overloaded flat directory that mixes bootstrap lifecycle, runtime context access, persistence, and feature-specific config concerns.
evidence: directory_profiles reports goseg/config/ with 41 files, exceeding the >10 mixed-concern split signal.

bootstrap.go owns initialization/bootstrap sequencing (`Initialize`, env/base-path/debug boot setup) while config.go exposes runtime context snapshots and setting wrappers, indicating distinct ownership boundaries in one directory.
Tests mirror this split concern (`bootstrap_test.go` validates process bootstrap/path/debug behavior) but remain in the same flat folder.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create target folders `goseg/config/bootstrap/`, `goseg/config/runtime/`, `goseg/config/state/`; (2) move bootstrap lifecycle code/tests first (`bootstrap.go`, `bootstrap_test.go`) into `config/bootstrap`; (3) move runtime-context facade types/functions from `config.go` into `config/runtime`; (4) keep compatibility shims in `goseg/config` temporarily and deprecate; (5) run import updates and verification with `go test ./goseg/config/...` and `go test ./...` after each stage, plus `rg "groundseg/config" goseg -g'*.go'` to update stale imports incrementally.

findings.summary: Lifecycle wait wrappers are placed in the broad shipworkflow package despite being delegated entirely to lifecyclewait, creating a boundary leak.
evidence: lifecycle.go imports `groundseg/shipworkflow/lifecyclewait`, aliases its types/errors, and forwards calls (`WaitForUrbitStopWith`, `WaitForUrbitStop`) with only nil-check glue.
doc.go states generic lifecycle primitives are intentionally owned by shared packages, but lifecycle wrappers remain in package root.
lifecycle_test.go validates wrapper behavior in root package rather than colocating with lifecyclewait-focused APIs.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create `goseg/shipworkflow/compat/` (or move callers directly to `lifecyclewait` if API stability allows); (2) move lifecycle wrapper + test to `compat` as temporary compatibility surface; (3) update call sites to import `groundseg/shipworkflow/lifecyclewait` directly where possible; (4) deprecate and remove compat after migration window; (5) validate with `go test ./goseg/shipworkflow/...` then `go test ./...`, and use `rg "WaitForUrbitStop\(|ErrShipStatusNotFound" goseg -g'*.go'` to ensure imports/callers are updated.

findings.summary: Container-specific runtime bridging/config assembly sits in orchestration root instead of a dedicated container-composition boundary.
evidence: directory_profiles reports goseg/docker/orchestration/ has 34 files, another flat overloaded directory above threshold.
container_bridge.go is primarily adapter/wiring code translating `dockerRuntime` to `container.*Runtime` templates.
container_config.go centralizes container builder registry and typed/string dispatch; this is composition-layer code distinct from generic orchestration runtime/status concerns.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create target folders `goseg/docker/orchestration/containerbridge/` and `goseg/docker/orchestration/containerconfig/` (or a combined `containercompose/`); (2) move `container_bridge.go` and `container_config.go` first, then tests; (3) leave thin forwarding functions in old package paths during transition; (4) update imports across orchestration callers; (5) validate with `go test ./goseg/docker/orchestration/...` and `go test ./...`, plus `rg "ContainerConfigForType|llamaRuntimeFromDocker|minioRuntimeFromDocker" goseg/docker -g'*.go'` for residual references.

findings.summary: Contract family declarations use separate data models without a single enforced architecture contract.
evidence: `family_specs_test.go` validates `contractFamilyEntrySpec`/catalog governance paths, but `protocolfamily/specs.go` and `startramfamily/specs.go` define separate `ActionSpec`/`ContractSpec` models.
Both family packages carry contract IDs and owner metadata as independent string constants, creating parallel authority for governance data.
findings.suggestion: Create one canonical family-spec schema in `goseg/protocol/contracts` (shared struct + validator), then make `protocolfamily` and `startramfamily` producers of that schema only. Remove duplicated model types and route all family declarations through one compile-time checked conversion path before catalog/binding registration.

findings.summary: Governance tests validate synthetic specs but miss regression coverage for real family declarations.
evidence: `family_specs_test.go` constructs local sample specs instead of loading `ActionSpecs()`/`ContractSpecs()` from family packages.
`fixtures_test.go` and `actions_test.go` do not bridge parser/binding behavior to governance family declarations, leaving compatibility drift undetected.
findings.suggestion: Add contract-integration tests that: (1) load all real family declarations, (2) assert each declared action/error maps to canonical catalog/bindings, and (3) assert parser-accepted actions (`uploadsvc`) resolve to governed contract entries. Keep existing unit tests, but add this cross-module suite as a required quality gate.

findings.summary: Family contract files are split across root and micro-packages, obscuring ownership and change boundaries.
evidence: README sets backend quality-gate expectations, but contract-governance code is dispersed across `contracts/` root tests and separate family leaf packages with different local schemas.
`directory_profiles` shows `goseg/protocol/contracts/` already has 11 mixed files, and family package placement is not reflected consistently in structure metadata, reducing navigability.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: Stage 1: create `goseg/protocol/contracts/families/protocol/` and `goseg/protocol/contracts/families/startram/` with a shared `families/spec.go` schema in `goseg/protocol/contracts/families/`. Stage 2: move `protocolfamily/specs.go` and `startramfamily/specs.go` into those targets and rename local types to the shared schema. Stage 3: move/adjust family-governance tests to `goseg/protocol/contracts/families/...` so tests are colocated with declarations. Stage 4: run import updates and validation commands: `cd goseg && gofmt -w ./protocol/contracts/... && go test ./protocol/contracts/... ./uploadsvc/... && go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Configuration domain is duplicated across persistence structs and patch schema layers, increasing drift risk and weakening a single source of truth.
evidence: `SysConfig` and its section structs define canonical configuration fields in `goseg/structs/configs.go`, while `goseg/config/config_update_schema.go` redefines many of the same concepts as pointer-based patch structs (`ConnectivityPatch`, `RuntimePatch`, `StartramPatch`, etc.).

The patch system then adds separate parse/apply/merge orchestration (`configPatchField`, typed parser/accessor plumbing, registry init/validation), creating a second structural model for the same domain.

Tests in `goseg/config/config_test.go` include extensive merge/default/migration behavior validation, which indicates this mapping layer is complex enough to require broad behavioral regression coverage.
findings.suggestion: Define a single field registry owned by the canonical config model (section + key + parse/apply/merge hooks) and generate or derive patch handlers from that registry so field additions/renames cannot diverge between `structs` and update schema code.

findings.summary: The orchestration runtime seam file aggregates too many subsystem responsibilities into one broad dependency surface.
evidence: `runtime_ops.go` declares many runtime groups (container, snapshot, health, startram, startup, urbit, context, file, image, volume, command, timer, wireguard, netdata, minio) plus multiple default constructors and a central seam registry.

Some responsibilities overlap across seam groups (for example repeated config snapshot concerns and duplicated copy-file seam shape in command/wireguard contexts), signaling boundary blur rather than strict module ownership.
High-level tests in `startuporchestrator` and `main` validate broad callback wiring, which reflects the large coordination blast radius created by the centralized runtime seam composition.
findings.suggestion: Split runtime seam composition by bounded context (startup bootstrap, container lifecycle, urbit management, networking, config snapshots) with narrower interfaces per workflow, then compose those modules at a higher bootstrap layer instead of one omnibus seam file.

findings.summary: State containers blend storage, event transport, and integration wiring, making each type responsible for multiple behavioral axes.
evidence: `broadcastStateRuntime` handles synchronized state storage, schedule queue publication, collector dependency resolution, and JSON envelope helpers in one module.
`logstreamSessionStore` owns session tracking for two domains (system and docker) and also message bus publication (`SystemLogMessages`, `PublishSystemLog`) in the same type.
`goseg/broadcast/state_test.go` focuses heavily on cloning/mutation safety behavior, suggesting this multi-role state object carries nontrivial invariants that are hard to isolate.
findings.suggestion: Separate pure state stores from event buses and dependency adapters: keep minimal state mutation/query types, move publish/scheduling concerns into dedicated coordinator components, and inject collectors/adapters through orchestration code.

findings.summary: Large test files repeatedly build local mini-harnesses and setup utilities, indicating missing shared test abstractions for recurring runtime/setup patterns.
evidence: `importer_test.go` defines multiple small runtime/setup helpers (`importerRuntimeWith`, `initRuntime`, `uploadRuntime`, `provisionRuntime`, session reset/auth setup helpers) alongside many scenario tests in a single file.
`accesspoint_test.go` repeats extensive command-flow/error-path matrix setup with custom global-reset helpers and command capture logic in one very large file.

`startup_orchestrator_test.go` and `wifi_test.go` also define custom local builder wrappers (`with...`/runtime helper functions), showing repeated structural test harness patterns across packages rather than shared utilities.
findings.suggestion: Introduce package-level reusable scenario builders/test fixtures (runtime override builders, command expectation DSLs, shared state reset helpers) and split current mega test files by behavior slices to reduce repeated harness code and clarify responsibility per test unit.

findings.summary: Process-wide singleton runtimes create order-dependent startup/test behavior.
evidence: defaultBroadcastStateRuntime and defaultBroadcastLoopController are package globals used by default Start/Stop paths.
defaultEventRuntime is built via sync.OnceValue and returned globally.
actions package memoizes contract maps with sync.Once and package-level mutable maps, binding behavior to first initialization state.
findings.suggestion: Replace hidden global defaults with an explicit app-level runtime container passed through startup wiring, and add explicit resettable test harness constructors so lifecycle state is controlled per test/process boundary.

findings.summary: Error contract style is inconsistent across related action/service APIs.
evidence: uploadsvc tests assert `err.Error()` exact strings for validation errors and propagated failures.
protocol/actions tests assert typed errors with errors.As (UnsupportedActionError).
wifi tests assert sentinel compatibility with errors.Is (ErrWiFiInterfaceNotFound).
findings.suggestion: Define exported typed/sentinel errors for upload command validation and dispatch, wrap with `%w`, and migrate tests/callers from string equality to errors.Is/errors.As so all subsystems share stable failure contracts.

findings.summary: Public APIs mix injected-runtime and implicit-global execution models.
evidence: shipworkflow exposes both override-aware and implicit-default functions for similar operations.
broadcast loop has StartBroadcastLoopWithRuntime but StopBroadcastLoop only targets global default controller.
events exposes NewEventRuntime(overrides...) alongside DefaultEventRuntime singleton path.
findings.suggestion: Standardize on one public pattern: either explicit runtime object methods everywhere or paired `WithRuntime` variants for all lifecycle operations (including stop/teardown paths), and deprecate implicit-global entrypoints.

findings.summary: Core config models contain mixed naming and JSON style islands.
evidence: ConnectivityConfig includes `ApiVersion` and `WgOn` while sibling fields use different acronym casing conventions.
RuntimeConfig has uppercase JSON tag `CFG_DIR` while nearby fields use camelCase tags.
The same file mixes camelCase system config tags with snake_case urbit config tags, creating convention drift at the primary config boundary.
findings.suggestion: Introduce explicit versioned persistence DTOs for legacy wire tags and normalize domain model naming (API/WG/CFG conventions) in one style; keep backward compatibility through marshal/unmarshal adapters rather than mixed in-model conventions.

findings.summary: Runtime seam names use inconsistent suffixes and abbreviation vocabulary.
evidence: orchestration runtime structs mix plain verbs (`Initialize`) with callback suffix forms (`...Fn`) in the same API layer.
Acronym usage varies by symbol and package (`Wg`, `Api`, `Cfg`, `MDNS`), reducing predictability when searching or extending APIs.
startup orchestrator uses strict `...Fn` callback naming, but neighboring runtime surfaces do not, producing cross-package naming drift.
findings.suggestion: Adopt a single seam naming convention (for example all injected callbacks end with `Fn`, and acronyms use canonical uppercase forms), then refactor runtime structs and call sites with a compatibility shim period for downstream packages/tests.
